figures.caption: Fig. 4: Qualitative Comparison. Top: Comparison with state-of-the-art methods. Bottom: Comparison between our 3DRAE and 3DDiT to demonstrate the effectiveness of diffusion modeling. The red bounding boxes highlight failure cases in baselines.
Fig. 8: Generated 3D Scenes under Single-View Conditioning. The red bounding boxes denote the conditional views. Generated point clouds are shown on the right.

Table 1: Quantitative Comparison. We compare both generation-oriented (1-view) and reconstruction-oriented (2-view) settings with state-of-the-art regression- (DepthSplat, LVSM) and diffusion-based (MotionCtrl, ViewCrafter, SEVA) methods. 2-view setting is not available (N/A) for MotionCtrl that only supports 1-view conditioning.

Fig. 2: We empirically find that overlapping regions across multiple views correspond to the same set of tokens. Therefore, we employ fixed-length 3D latent tokens to eliminate such multi-view redundancy.

Fig. 12: Representing Densely Observed 3D Scenes using 3DRAE. Our 3DRAE can represent the same 3D scenes but observed by different numbers of views (16 vs. 128 views) using the same set of 1K tokens. The red arrows highlight conditioning camera trajectories that specify the spatial extents of the generated scenes.
Table 5: Details of 3DRAE architecture and hyperparameters.

Fig. 11: Zero-Shot Generated 3D Scenes under Single-View Conditioning. Rows 1∼2: results on Tanks and Temples dataset [33]. Rows 3∼4: results on MipNeRF 360 dataset [4]. Rows 5∼6: results on DTU dataset [1]. The red bounding boxes denote the conditional views. Generated point clouds are shown on the right.
Table 6: Details of 3DDiT architecture and hyperparameters.
Fig. 9: Generated 3D Scenes under Sparse-View Conditioning. The red bounding boxes denote the conditional views. Generated point clouds are shown on the right.
Fig. 10: Generated 3D Scenes without Appearance Conditioning. Using the same camera trajectory condition with different random seeds, we generate 3D scenes with varied appearances.
